nativeRange.habitat: Reef-associated environments at 15–120 m depth; typically found in caves or among coral rubble along steep drop-offs, channel walls, and outer reef slopes

decorPreferences: live rock with caves and crevices

diet.notes: Primarily a planktivore in the wild, feeding on zooplankton. In captivity, has been documented accepting frozen mysis shrimp and enriched frozen brine shrimp; over time may accept high-quality flake foods. A refugium seeding live copepods and amphipods is generally considered beneficial. Multiple small feedings per day are advised due to a high metabolic rate.

summary: A small, colorful reef anthias from the Pacific, best kept as a harem (one male with several females) in a well-established system. Males display red and magenta striping; females have a distinctive yellow nose.
careNotes:
Pseudanthias randalli is a protogynous hermaphrodite — all individuals are born female, and the dominant individual in a group will transition to male if no male is present. Males display vivid red and magenta striping; females are distinguished by yellow snout coloration. If the dominant male perishes, the largest female in the group has been observed morphing to take its place.

Males are generally best kept singly or alongside multiple females; keeping more than one male in the same system tends to result in conspecific aggression. A group of one male with three or more females is commonly recommended, requiring a tank of at least 500 L to provide adequate territory.

This species tends to dwell toward the lower portions of the water column and appreciates the availability of several hiding places in the rockwork. A refugium producing live copepods and amphipods is widely considered beneficial as a food supplement.

Feeding should occur multiple times per day in small portions; the species has been reported to have a high metabolic rate and may decline if underfed. Frozen mysis shrimp and enriched frozen brine shrimp are the most commonly cited staple foods; transitioning individuals to prepared flake foods can take time and patience.

Like many anthias, this species has been known to jump from open aquaria; a secure, tight-fitting lid is advisable.

Adult size disagreement between sources: LiveAquaria cites a maximum of 3 in (approximately 7.6 cm), while FishBase records a maximum total length of 9.5 cm. Values averaged to a range of 5–9 cm for this entry. No lifespan data was available from either primary or secondary source.

Temperature disagreement: LiveAquaria cites 72–78°F (22.2–25.6°C); FishBase cites 22–27°C. Values averaged to 22–26°C for this entry.
breedingNotes: Captive breeding is considered very difficult. As a protogynous hermaphrodite, sex change is socially mediated — removing or losing the dominant male triggers the largest female to transition. Larval rearing in home aquariums has not been widely reported for this species.

additional.name: FishBase
additional.notes: Cross-checked adult size (max 9.5 cm TL vs LiveAquaria 3 in / 7.6 cm — averaged to 5–9 cm range), temperature range (22–27°C vs LiveAquaria 72–78°F / 22.2–25.6°C — averaged to 22–26°C), taxonomy (Family Anthiadidae, Order Perciformes), and native range (Philippines to Niue, Ryukyu Islands, depths 15–120 m). Reef2Reef wiki returned HTTP 403; BlueZoo Aquatics did not have a species-level page for this species.

fish.breedingNotes: Protogynous hermaphrodite; sex change is socially triggered. Captive spawning has not been widely reported for this species; larval rearing in home aquariums is considered very challenging.
